params.text:
Safe storage, use, labelling and disposal

How you handle a substance across its whole life matters, not just when you are using it. Sensible habits keep everyone safe.

  • Storage – keep products in a locked, ventilated cupboard away from food, out of reach of the people you support, and never near heat or naked flames.
  • Use – follow the label and assessment, use the right amount, and never mix products (especially bleach with acids or other cleaners).
  • Labelling – always keep substances in their original, labelled containers. Never decant into an unmarked bottle or a drinks container.
  • Disposal – dispose of waste as instructed; put clinical waste in the correct coloured bags and sharps in a sharps bin. Do not pour hazardous chemicals down the drain.

Dealing with spillages

A spill can create fumes, a slip hazard or a chemical burn, so act calmly and safely. Check the safety data sheet, which sets out how to deal with a spill of that particular product.

  • Warn others and, if needed, keep people away from the area.
  • Put on the correct PPE before touching anything.
  • Use the right spill kit or absorbent material; never mix a spilled chemical with another product.
  • Ventilate the area if there are fumes.
  • Clean up, dispose of the waste correctly, and report the spill to your manager.

params.question: You find a cleaning chemical that has been decanted into an unlabelled squash bottle in the kitchen. What is the safest action?

answers.text: Do not use it, keep it away from others, and report it to your manager straight away

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Unlabelled substances are dangerous because no one knows what they are or how to handle them, and someone could drink it by mistake.

answers.text: Smell it to work out which product it is, then carry on using it

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Smelling an unknown chemical could expose you to harmful fumes, and it does not make the container safe or correctly labelled.

answers.text: Leave it where it is so the person who filled it can deal with it later

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Leaving an unlabelled chemical in a drinks bottle in a kitchen is a serious risk that must be dealt with immediately.

answers.text: Pour it down the sink to get rid of the problem

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Pouring an unknown chemical down the drain can harm the environment and may react dangerously; report it instead.
